intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

body.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

draft.title: recipe app for people who hate cooking
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
recipe app for people who hate cooking

Building your first draft…

data.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking respond better to a voice that's irreverent and funny rather than instructional or encouraging
facade.label: Cheerful sass
facade.acceptImplies: The app's core personality should be witty, self-aware, and unafraid to mock both cooking and the user's reluctance—like a sarcastic friend in the kitchen
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer a more neutral, straightforward, or genuinely encouraging tone that respects their aversion without making light of it

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking respond better to warm, low-light interfaces that feel less clinical and more forgiving—reducing anxiety around the cooking task itself.
facade.label: Dark & cozy
facade.acceptImplies: Explore darker palettes, warm accent colors (amber, terracotta), soft shadows, and a 'kitchen at night' aesthetic that makes the app feel intimate rather than instructional.
facade.rejectImplies: Move toward bright, clean, high-contrast layouts that feel professional and clear—the app should be a confident guide, not a cozy retreat.

evidence.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking respond better to a voice that's irreverent and funny rather than instructional or encouraging
evidence.implication: The app's core personality should be witty, self-aware, and unafraid to mock both cooking and the user's reluctance—like a sarcastic friend in the kitchen

facade.hypothesis: For a 'people who hate cooking' app, reducing friction at the ingredient stage—making items fade/disappear as you add them rather than stack in a list—feels less overwhelming and more rewarding than a static checklist.
facade.label: Ingredients dissolve in
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers satisfying, motion-based feedback for task completion; may favor dopamine-hit design (quick victories, visual feedback).
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers clarity and list persistence; may favor predictable, static layouts where all steps remain visible and scannable.

facade.hypothesis: A recipe app for cooking-averse users works better with large, scannable cards showing one recipe each, rather than dense lists or grids.
facade.axisTargeted: Information density and card vs. list layout
facade.label: Giant recipe cards
facade.acceptImplies: Users prefer spacious, image-forward cards that feel less overwhelming and more like browsing than scrolling through text. This signals that reducing cognitive load matters more than showing many options at once.
facade.rejectImplies: Users want to see more recipes at a glance, compare options quickly, or prefer a compact layout. This suggests density and efficiency are valued over spaciousness, or that large cards feel wasteful for recipe discovery.

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking prefer minimal cognitive load — they want to know WHAT to make before seeing ingredient lists, steps, or nutritional data
facade.axisTargeted: Information density & sequencing — do users want the recipe name/decision isolated first, or recipe + key metric (time, difficulty, servings) shown together as a unit?
facade.label: Just the recipe title
facade.acceptImplies: Lead with recipe name only. Delay ingredient/step details to a second screen or collapsible section. Users are decision-makers first, readers second.
facade.rejectImplies: Bundle the recipe title with 1–2 key metrics (cook time, difficulty) on the card or landing view. Users need context to decide, not just a name.

facade.hypothesis: A recipe app for cooking-averse users works best with a bright, encouraging tone and quick-win interaction patterns rather than heavy, intimidating interfaces.
facade.label: Cheerful shortcuts
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward interfaces that feel like a supportive friend cheering them on. Design should lean playful, use uplifting language, celebrate small steps. Rewards and encouragement over minimalist zen.
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers a cooler, more neutral or even stark aesthetic. Efficiency and clarity matter more than charm. Interface should stay out of the way. They may find cheerfulness cloying or patronizing in a cooking context.

axes.label: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
Whether user wants minimal decision-making (curated picks) vs. maximum filtering control (dietary, time, skill filters)
Whether user prefers instant gratification (no loading, snappy) vs. exploratory browsing with smooth transitions and discovery

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants minimal decision-making (curated picks) vs. maximum filtering control (dietary, time, skill filters)
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers instant gratification (no loading, snappy) vs. exploratory browsing with smooth transitions and discovery
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking may actually want to feel guided and reassured by a competent, take-charge voice rather than a peer-like sarcastic one—valuing capability over personality
facade.axisTargeted: Voice tone: irreverent peer vs. authoritative expert
facade.label: Bossy AI assistant
facade.acceptImplies: Double down on commanding, confident language that removes decision-making burden ('Here's what you're making'); users crave competence over charm
facade.rejectImplies: Irreverent sass hypothesis holds; users want to feel entertained and in control, not managed by the app

draft.title: QuickBite – Recipe App for People Who Hate Cooking
draft.summary: A recipe app that strips away complexity and delivers dead-simple meals in under 15 minutes with minimal ingredients and zero fuss.
draft.html:
QuickBite

Meals so simple, you'll actually make them

Today's Picks

Garlic Butter Pasta

8 min4 ingredients

Boil pasta. Toss with butter, garlic, salt. Done. No sauce needed.

Egg Fried Rice

12 min5 ingredients

Rice, eggs, soy sauce, frozen veg. Stir in a pan. Dinner solved.

Avocado Toast

5 min3 ingredients

Toast bread. Mash avocado. Spread. Salt. Eat. Breakfast champion.

Stuck?

Get a random recipe that takes 10 minutes or less
